title: Chapter 40: The Path
content: The light wakes me before the alarm does.

That's the thing about Irish summer — the mornings arrive like they've been waiting all night for permission, golden and urgent and gone too fast. I've lived in Clonakilty for three years now and I still haven't gotten used to it. The way the sun hits the window at 5:47 a.m., slanting through the gap in the curtains, painting the ceiling in a shade of amber that doesn't exist anywhere else. Brief and precious, like everything good in this country.

Julian's side of the bed is empty. The sheets are still warm where he was, and I can hear him downstairs — the kitchen tap running, the espresso machine building pressure with that low, determined hiss that sounds like a small dragon waking up. The routine is so familiar it's practically choreographed: tap, grind, hiss, pour. Julian Gallagher's morning symphony in D minor.
I would lie here and enjoy it, except there is a twelve-pound cat on my chest.

Gatsby is sitting on my sternum with the particular intensity of a creature who has decided that breakfast is not merely overdue but constitutionally offensive. His yellow eyes are approximately two inches from my face. His expression communicates, with remarkable clarity for a being who lacks language: *How dare you sleep when I am hungry.*
"Good morning to you too," I say.

He meows. It's the meow of profound betrayal, of a cat who has been abandoned and neglected and starved — or at least made to wait an additional forty-five minutes past his scheduled 6:00 a.m. feeding. I push him off, gently, and he slides onto the mattress with the exaggerated grace of a fallen aristocrat, giving me a look that could curdle milk.
I give him a look back. *You'll survive.*
Same conversation we have every morning. Same lovely, ordinary, completely unextraordinary conversation. I wouldn't trade it for anything.

I pull on Julian's hoodie — the green one that's technically his but has lived on my side of the room for the better part of a year — and pad barefoot down the stairs. The café is dark and silent, chairs up on tables, the cookie case empty, the chalk menu board still showing yesterday's specials in Julian's blocky handwriting. Closed today. Our first full day off in two weeks, and the silence of the place without customers feels like a held breath, the way a school feels on a weekend — full of latent energy, waiting to be needed again.
I follow the smell of coffee into the kitchen, and there it is: a scone on the counter, still warm, split and buttered the way I like it. Next to it, a handwritten note on the back of a receipt.
*For the woman who chose me across three universes. Happy anniversary.*
I pick up the note. I read it twice. Three times.

It's not our wedding anniversary — that's in October, and it involves significantly more fanfare and significantly better catering. It's not the anniversary of the Choice either, the day I picked this timeline and let the others go. We don't celebrate that one. It's not something to celebrate; it's something to acknowledge, like a scar that finally stopped aching.
It's the anniversary of the day I found the Fork app.
The day everything started.

He remembers. Of course he remembers. Julian remembers everything — not the way I remember things, which is in fragments and feelings and the half-crumpled shape of dreams, but the way a cartographer remembers terrain. He filed away that date and decided it mattered and woke up early on a day off to make me a scone and write a note that makes my chest tight in the best possible way.
I eat the scone standing at the counter, still holding the note.

It's perfect. Three years of practice and he's finally beaten my grandmother's recipe — the right ratio of butter to flour, the crumb that holds together but yields, the faint sweetness that doesn't try too hard. I will never, ever tell him this. His scones are already an ego risk; if he knew he'd surpassed my grandmother, the woman who once told a Michelin-starred chef that his soda bread was "fine, I suppose," he'd be insufferable. I'd never hear the end of it. Every scone would come with a preamble.

So I eat it in silent appreciation, the warmth and the butter and the perfect balance of it, and I drink my coffee — the espresso he pulled, cut with just enough hot water, sitting in the ceramic mug I bought at the Kinsale market because it had a picture of a sheep on it and I thought it was funny. The coffee is good. The scone is better. The note is best.
And then I look out the window.

The garden is in full summer riot — the lavender I didn't kill is buzzing with bees, the rosemary is climbing the stone wall like it's trying to escape, and the workshop space beyond it, the one Julian built last summer with reclaimed timber and more optimism than structural engineering, is glowing in the morning light. Beyond the garden, beyond the fields that slope away from the house, the sea is a thin bright line of silver, the way it always looks from this angle, like someone laid a mirror at the edge of the world.

I look at all of it. The garden. The workshop. The sea. The scone crumbs on the counter. The cat who has followed me downstairs and is now circling my ankles with the single-minded purpose of a shark.
And I realize: I'm not thinking about other timelines.

I'm not wondering what-if. I'm not running mental simulations of the lives I didn't take, the cities I didn't live in, the choices I didn't make. I'm not standing in one world with my mind split across three. I'm just — here. Present. The way that meditation app kept telling me to be, back when I was a person who couldn't sit still for thirty seconds without mentally renovating my entire existence. The way I never could be before.
The moment is small and ordinary and it tastes like butter and coffee and it sounds like bees and it smells like rosemary and the sea, and I am completely, unambiguously in it.
The back door opens, and Julian walks in.

His hands are dirty — proper dirty, dark soil packed under his fingernails, brown smudges across his knuckles. There's a streak of it on his cheek where he must have pushed his hair back. He's wearing the faded grey t-shirt with the coffee stain on the hem that I keep threatening to throw away and he keeps rescuing from the donation pile.
And in his fingers, held with the careful gentleness of someone presenting something fragile: a single yellow rose.

The yellow rose. The one I planted two years ago in a fit of optimism, the kind of optimism that comes from living with a man who makes you believe you might actually be capable of keeping a plant alive. I am not, historically, a person who keeps plants alive. I am a person who kills succulents — succulents, the metaphor for resilience, the plant equivalent of a cockroach — and yet this rose bush has somehow survived under my sporadic, over-attentive care. I water it too much and talk to it and occasionally play it classical music because I read somewhere that plants like that, and Julian pretends not to notice, and the rose bush thrives out of what I can only assume is pity.
He holds it out to me.
"For the window," he says.

I take it. The petals are soft and cool under my fingers, that particular velvet texture that roses have, like silk that learned how to breathe. The yellow is vivid without being loud — warm, buttery, the same gold as the morning light. I smell it, even though yellow roses don't have much scent, and there it is: that faint green-sweet whisper, the promise of a fragrance that exists more in memory than in the actual blossom.

I get a mason jar from the shelf — the shelf of mason jars, because we run a café and we go through a lot of preserves — and fill it with water and set the rose in it and put it on the windowsill. Yellow rose in a mason jar on a kitchen counter in a cat café in West Cork.
This is my life.
This is the life I chose.
This is the only life I want.

Julian washes his hands at the sink, the water running brown then clear, and he dries them on the tea towel and comes to stand behind me, his chin on my shoulder, his arms around my waist. He smells like garden dirt and coffee and that cedar soap he's been buying since before I met him. I lean back into him.
"You like the rose?" he asks, even though he already knows the answer because he is Julian and he always knows.
"I like the rose," I confirm.
"The bush is doing well this year."
"The bush is doing well because I serenade it with Debussy."

He laughs — the real laugh, the one that rumbles through his chest and into my back and makes the whole morning feel like it was designed specifically to make me happy. Which, given the scone and the note and the rose, it possibly was.

He kisses the top of my head and goes back outside, because the garden won't weed itself, and because Julian Gallagher is fundamentally incapable of sitting still when there is visible plant disorder. I watch him go — the set of his shoulders, the way he moves through the garden like he belongs to it, the coffee-stain t-shirt getting farther away and smaller.
I sit on the window bench.

The same bench. The same window bench where I ate breakfast that first morning in Clonakilty, when the café was a hiding place and Julian was a stranger with kind eyes and sure hands and a voice like midnight radio, and I was running from a choice I hadn't made yet, running so hard I didn't realize I'd already started to stop.

I used to sit here and think about all the ways my life could go wrong. All the paths that led to disaster. All the branching possibilities that kept me awake at 3 a.m., paralyzed by the terror of picking the wrong one. Now I sit here and think about the rose. About the espresso machine. About whether Gatsby has been fed, because he's back to circling my ankles like a furry subpoena, and apparently I have to be the one to do it because Julian is outside and someone in this household needs to advocate for this cat's very urgent, very immediate, very life-threatening hunger.
I feed Gatsby. He attacks the bowl like he hasn't eaten in a fortnight. I return to the bench.

My left hand rests on my knee, and I touch the ring. The Claddagh ring — Julian's grandmother's, resized for my hand, worn every day since he gave it to me on a beach in Galway with the wind trying to kill us both and his hair standing straight up and his voice somehow steady through all of it.
Hands for friendship. Heart for love. Crown for loyalty.
The Fork app was three paths. This ring is one.

That's the whole story, really. Three paths become one. The garden becomes a garden. The labyrinth becomes a home. The girl who was afraid of choosing becomes the woman who chose and kept choosing, every day, in every small way that choosing happens — coffee or tea, stay or go, this life or another — until choosing stopped being scary and started being the whole point.
I reach for my laptop.

Not for the café — the accounts can wait, the inventory can wait, everything can wait because today is our day off and I am going to use it. Not for code, not for slide decks, not for startup pitches or investment summaries or any of the thousand urgent documents I spent years of my life creating and deleting and recreating like they mattered.
For myself.

I've started writing. Not well — I'm under no illusions about that. The sentences come out clunky and overwrought and I have to rewrite every paragraph three times before it sounds like something a human would actually say. But they come. Stories. I'm writing a story about a girl who lived three lives and chose one and found that one was enough. It's fiction, technically. But you know.

I type. The keyboard clicks under my fingers, that satisfying plastic tap-tap-tap that sounds like progress, like something being built. The words are slow today, the way they often are — I'm at the beginning, the hardest part, the part where the whole story is a fist that hasn't unclenched yet. But I work through it. I find the thread. I follow it.
And then, finally, the last line of the first chapter comes clear:
*I used to think choosing meant losing everything else. Now I know: choosing means finding what was always yours.*

I read it back. It's right. It's the most right thing I've written. It's the thesis of everything — the three years, the three lives, the fear and the fork and the falling and the finding. Not a lesson learned once and done. A truth that I have to keep choosing, every morning, every scone, every yellow rose.
I close the laptop.

Julian is in the garden, weeding between the lavender, his back to me, the grey t-shirt dark with sweat between his shoulder blades. Gatsby is on the windowsill, front paws tucked, eyes half-closed, judging everyone and everything with the serene superiority of a creature who has never once doubted that the universe was arranged for his convenience. The sea is shining beyond the fields, silver and blue and impossibly wide.

And somewhere — somewhere in the deep architecture of reality, in the quantum infrastructure that underpins everything, in the garden where all paths fork and all choices live and all the lives I didn't take go on without me — there are books on a shelf I'll never read. Gorgeous, unread, alive with their own stories. The Avery who stayed in San Francisco. The Avery who chased the IPO. The Averys I was and wasn't and could have been, each of them real in the way that all unbuilt houses are real — possible, complete in their possibility, unvisited.
I'm glad they exist. I'm glad I don't need to visit them.
I already know how they end.

They end the same way this one does: with a woman who finally stopped being afraid of choosing. With a man who helped her choose. With a life that was one of infinite possibilities — and that made it the most precious thing in any world.
The morning light shifts. The rose turns gold in the jar. Gatsby closes his eyes.
And I am here. I am home. I am exactly where I chose to be.
THE END
